speaker: interviewer
text: I'm all set.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you need more water?

speaker: interviewer
text: Some tissues?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think I need some tissues, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. they're--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. They're in here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Maybe I'll take two too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. I didn't know that. I didn't know that you had tissues here, so-- Oh. OK. Oh. I'm going to need those. I have--

speaker: interviewer
text: See what you're doing to us?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel--

speaker: interviewer
text: She's over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I'm ready whenever you are.

speaker: interviewer
text: You need one?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I got you some hankies.

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm OK. I'm all set.

speaker: interviewer
text: You all right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: I read in a certain book that there were 15,000-- 15,000 Yiddish periodicals at the onset of the war. Isn't that amazing?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's probably very true, because in our city of Radom, which had a population of about 30,000 Jews, there must've been at least five or six dailies plus some monthlies. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: It's unbelievable.

speaker: interviewee
text: Unbelievable, yes. The level of involvement of the Jewish community was very great, very, very, very, very aware, very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you know, there were three famous Jewish photographers in the middle and late 19th century? I thought that was amazing to learn. I mean, you know, just active-- [SIGHS] active community.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] start talking. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Sneaky. Mr. Horn, we were talking about your summer vacations in Pionki. What year would that be in your recollection?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We were talking about '36, '37, '38--

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you--

speaker: interviewer
text: And '39.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you stop going to Pionki?

speaker: interviewee
text: At the onset of the war, so our last year in Pionki was summer of 1939.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then what happened? What-- what restrictions were on your life in Radom that weren't there before the September 1st, 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, let me describe to you the-- the war, how I faced it and what happened. In 1939, I was 12 years old. And I remember the first-- the most outstanding thing to my memory is the Stuka attacks that came over our heads. And I remember distinctly how they swooped down. There was a meadow near our house. And there was a milkman that we used to get milk from, with three children. And he was out there in the meadow with three of his cows. And the Stukas came down. And if you watch slow motion sport events sometimes, this is what it looked like to me, that they came down, they killed all three cows. They killed him and they killed his children. That was my introduction to the war. And it probably happened on September 2nd, probably either the first or the second day of the war. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After that, we spent our days in bunkers or we spent our days with our face in the dirt that I remember. And there were seven days of constant bombardment, either by Stukas, low-level, low-flying fighter bombers, or the heavy Heinkels that dropped bombs on us. And we also heard the drone of planes overhead going on the way to Warsaw, to bomb Warsaw, which they leveled in those days. I only mention that because the Germans have a habit of reminding us that we bombed Dresden, you know, that we bombed Dresden. And-- and every time I read about their complaints about our bombing Dresden, I always think of the Stukas coming down and killing that poor milkman with his children and the cows. And so that was that we had seven days. On a Friday of the same week, a plane flew over-- overhead, over the city. And it left a-- a mark, which evidently was the sign to the ground troops that the city is empty, and it is ready to be entered. And after about a half hour, I heard a mighty roar coming in from the western side of the city. And we knew that the Germans were entering the city. And so I overcame my fright, all my emotions. And as a curious child, I ran to the road that I knew the Germans were coming in from. And so I got there. And I had to hold my hands over my head like that. And I watched a whole array of this great German might, tank after tank after tank with these tankers in their goggles, you know, with the-- with this tremendous might, with the long guns on their tanks. And that made a tremendous impression on me. I just thought that this kind of might will conquer the world, no ifs and buts about it. That's how powerful they loomed at the time. So that was my introduction to the entry of the German into the city. And then after a few days, a spate of laws came out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of them pertained to the general population, you know, like give up all radios, turn in all guns or all ammunitions. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And then the laws began to appear. They were on kiosks or just displayed in principal places. They were strictly against Jews, that Jews--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the laws have a name? Did they call them anything?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. All I remember is reading them off what they called the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. The [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] means announcements.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they ever referred to as the Nuremberg laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. No. They were-- these were just local laws that were promulgated by the occupation forces that entered the city. And they were probably following some kind of a schedule, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at first, Jews cannot do this. Jews cannot do that. All schooling for Jews was immediately forbidden. The synagogues were closed. You couldn't to go to pray anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: There are a spate of laws, dozens of them each day came up, limiting the ability to move around. There was a curfew-- the ability to, say, go to a bank and deposit money or take money out, you know. All these things that we take for granted, that we do every day, were-- were being limited by the authorities. And-- and that was the-- was the first. That was the easiest. That was first introduction to German occupation, to Nazi occupation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then after you saw the tanks, did you go home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. I did go home. I didn't say anything about it. I was too-- too shocked. I was too shocked.

speaker: interviewer
text: All I could think of is at the age of 12, why could I not be a part of such tremendous might? And I couldn't reconcile the fact that this might is coming into-- against me. It's coming in against us, because we knew that the Germans had no good intentions toward Jews. But these were the emotional feelings of a young boy, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] which could be overcome soon by other things. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Immediately, we felt the strain and we got restricted. We-- we could not operate the same way as we did before. My father couldn't go to work anymore. He lost his job. He lost his income. I remember my oldest brother Abram was able to go to the bank I think a day or two after they entered. And he was able to withdraw 500 zloties, which he had on his bank account. And for some reason he beat the clock, because I remember like a day or two later the announcement came out that Jews cannot withdraw money from banks. So he managed to withdraw the 500 zloties. I also remember that my father in his wisdom had brought home from the warehouse that he worked out of several packages of leather, soft leather, which his firm made. And he just brought it home for the first time that I can remember. And those several packages of leather became an underpinning for our-- for our survival, because money-- inflation ran up immediately high. And if you had money it became worthless pretty soon. I mean, it took maybe a couple months that money became almost worthless. And-- so anything that you had to barter, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] that was valuable. And these packages of leather served us to survive the first winter of the Nazi occupation. This happened in September. My father had these few packages of leather. They became-- you put could barter, let's say, one piece of leather for-- for three or four loaves of bread and stuff like that, so that we were able to-- we were able to do OK for that winter. We were able to go out and buy potatoes enough for the winter. I remember we bought pickles for the winter. These were staples. We bought enough coal for the winter to get us through the cold winter. We-- we bought enough staples so that we could continue our life semi-normal. In November of 1939, my brother Abram left the house and he went to the-- he went to the Polish-- to the eastern side of Poland, which was occupied by Russia. So this was the first member of our family that left us. And he insisted that he wanted to go. He went with a couple of his friends. And it was possible to do that at the time, because the border between Poland-- between the German-occupied Poland and Russian-occupied Poland was not well defined yet. And it was possible to cross over back and forth. And people-- a lot of people were doing that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my brother was one of them. And he went to a city called Luck on the eastern side of Poland, which was occupied by the Russians. And we got letters from him. He got a job as a teacher in a school. Could you spell Luck, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: L-U-C-K.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your other siblings?

speaker: interviewee
text: We remained home. My brother Eli stayed home and my sister Luma stayed home. And we all pitched in and helped my father do anything we could to earn for the sustenance we needed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long did this go on?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, because of the position that my father had and because of the fact that he worked for German owners, as I told you before, he was-- we were lucky in a way, because these Germans, even though they were ethnics-- they were German ethnics who lived in Poland even before the war-- they had lived in the town of Radom, the city of Radom for many years. And at first, just like nobody else, they didn't believe that Hitler is for real, you know. Everybody thought that once England and France get into war, they will take care of Hitler in one fell swoop. Of course, we know now it didn't happen that way. But in those days, even the Germans, the ethnic Germans, didn't believe that Hitler was going to make it. They thought it was only temporary. So they wanted to maintain their ties with the Jewish business community that they had before the war so that in case Hitler is gone and the war ends, they would still be able to function in our city.

speaker: interviewer
text: So things in the first year of occupation, the first year and a half I might say, were not as bad as they could have been. We were able to maintain our family and we did fairly well. We had enough to eat, and there was no question of-- of starvation or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: But when the Germans conquered France and Paris fell, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the attitude of these German bosses, as well as many other people who are so-called fence-sitters, changed. All of a sudden, everybody realized that Hitler  is for real, that the mighty French army was defeated by them in several weeks, so they all had a right to think that Hitler is invincible, that his armies are invincible. And in fact, at the time, they were. So all these people, these so-called political fence-sitters that we have in every country, toppled over to the side of the Nazis. And then things begin to get tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we're talking about after the spring of 1940.

speaker: interviewee
text: '40. Right. After the spring of 1940, things began to get tougher and tougher. Right. And that was the time when the laws were passed where we needed to wear the armbands with the stars. And many other laws were passed that continually tightened the noose, economic and political, around us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you in the ghetto at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think the ghetto was started in the beginning of 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: So could you describe further the changes and the effects on your life after May 1940, as you were speaking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you know, as soon as the war broke out and Jewish children were not allowed to go to school, my parents insisted that I join a clandestine school. Clandestine schools opened up. And as any society, you know, the important thing for Jewish people was always to get educated. So many clandestine schools opened up. And I joined one of them. There was a Miss Sobol that I remember. She was our teacher. She had been a teacher in the school that I attended before the war. And so we would meet, about 14, 15 children, in a high school class. We would meet in her room. And she would teach us. And so I continued to go to school. And in fact, any schooling that I have, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you might say high school, regular high school curriculum, stems from those days, because in those days when I went to school, I went in a very serious manner, because not only were my parents sacrificing a great deal, but we were also taking a chance with our lives. So this was serious business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was this during the day or at night?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was during the day.

speaker: interviewer
text: During the day.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was during the day. Serious business. So I applied myself with all the strength that I had. And I was never a good student before. Before the war, I was a very poor student. I could never apply myself to my studies. But during the war, I started to apply myself. And all of a sudden, I found that I have an ability to do this and that and the other. And-- and at- at- a  lot of times, Miss Sobol would call on me to give answers to questions that nobody else knew, and I somehow did.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was a period of my time when I learned a great deal. With Miss Sobol, I took German, I took French, and I took English. And-- and these-- and I also took Latin. And all these languages came in very handy in my later years as I needed them. So that was my own part, you know, but that had nothing to with my parents [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: My parents, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] you know, things got tougher and tougher. And as I said, these Germans, his bosses, dropped the association. And they would no longer have anything to do with us, because, you know, they thought that Hitler has got it, that that's going to be it. And in fact, it was very dangerous for them to have liaison with Jews also. I must add that. They kept this liaison for a while, even though-- even though they knew-- I suspect they knew what was in store for Jews. They probably knew that from conversations with their-- with their German friends, the occupiers. But they did kept- keep the liaison for some time, I guess because of-- of a friendship that my father had developed with them since they were children. But eventually, they dropped it. It was impossible to maintain it, because I remember we would go out in the middle of the night, outside the ghetto, and take off the armbands, which itself was a-- punished by death immediately. And then we would go to the factory, which was outside of town. And that's in the middle of the night, and I remember in the wintertime. This was the winter of 1941. And we would go to the factory. And they would let us in, these Germans. They-- they would let us into the factory. But they were so afraid. They were so scared. They couldn't wait for the minute we left. They gave us the leather and then we left. And-- and we knew that this wasn't going to continue, because if we got caught, they would torture us and make us say where we got the leather, so they would get implicated also. So the whole thing was just terrible, you know. And after a while, it-- we couldn't do it anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you stopped doing it, did your lifestyle have-- not your lifestyle.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I understand.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there restrictions on your food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. The restrictions-- the restrictions were evident more and more. More and more we had to restrict ourselves, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] until the time came when the rumors started, which was 1942 when the rumors started about deportations.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who did you hear the rumors from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. The deportations were-- I don't know who coined these phrases. I don't understand to this day who coined these phrases. But deportation was a euphemism for a one-way trip to nowhere. You understand that. But the word deportation denotes in my-- in my judgment, a change of address rather than going to a killing place.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these-- these things-- we heard of re-settlements. We heard of deportations. And the first action actually started in the city of Lublin. And it started in March of '42. That was the first city that was under the General Government, which means the Polish side of the occupation, that was emptied of Jews. And about 40,000 or 45,000 Jews perished from Lublin.

speaker: interviewee
text: But since the action that took place in March of '42 in Lublin, we started hearing these rumors, you know, that this city is next, and this city is next, and that they are emptying out and resettling or deporting Jews somewhere to the east, to the Ukraine. That was the-- that was the-- kind of a delusion, you know. People probably liked these names because they deluded themselves. Nobody could believe that anybody could take 30,000, 40,000 people out of a city and just do away with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you witness a deportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Eventually I witnessed deportation in Radom. But before that, when we heard these rumors, it also became known, and the Germans themselves [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] made sure that everybody in the ghetto knew, that there was a way of preventing resettlement or deportation. And the way to prevent re-settlement or deportation was to get an essential job with the Germans. The idea was if you get a war-essential job with the Germans, that you could remain and so could your family.

speaker: interviewer
text: On this basis, my brother Eli and I enlisted and went to work for-- in a munitions factory in Radom. What is more important for a war industry than munitions? So we went to work hoping that with this job, we will prevent them from resetting or deporting our family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, nothing could be further from the truth. It was just a ruse, and one of many the Germans used on us. And the next thing that I knew-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] my brother worked the day shift. I worked the night shift. We worked a 12-hour shift.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In this munitions factory in Radom. And, you know, Radom had been set up with two ghettos, one-- two areas. One was called Glinice, which had about 10,000 Jews in it. And one was called Walowa in another part of town where we lived, which had about 25,000 Jews. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] One day when we were finished with the night shift work, and it was about after 6:00 in the morning, about 6:30, we were ready to be escorted back into the ghetto. We were escorted with Ukrainian black-uniformed guards. All of a sudden, we found that there was a lot of shooting coming from the area of the ghetto. And the guards themselves became confused as to what to do, because they realized that there's something going on. Well, anyway, that night, which was August 5th, 1942, they had cleaned out the 10,000 Jews of the ghetto Glinice completely. They found that they still had some room on the trains. And so early in the morning, they went down to the other ghetto, to Walowa. And they started a selection procedure in Walowa and they took about 2,500 people from Walowa that day. So that first day, on August 5th, they took  10,000 Jews from Glinice and about 2,500 Jews from Walowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it an action?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was called-- yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: On August 5th.

speaker: interviewee
text: This was one of these deportations or  re-settlement actions, yes. When we came back that morning from my job at the munitions factory, I came face to face with a group of Jews that were taken out from the 2,500 who were taken out from the ghetto and marched to the railroad station, because the munitions factory was located near the railroad station. And so we actually-- when they brought us back to the ghetto, we actually came face to face with the people that were being taken out. And this is when I witnessed the first thing-- the first time a so-called deportation or re-settlement action. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It's very difficult for me to describe it. But [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we came on a large meadow. It was a large field. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All this was taking place in the outskirts of the city. They never took people through the main streets of the city. They always took the people-- even though it was a longer route, they took them out of the-- out of sight.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were in a-- in a large, empty field. And we came across [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] these columns of people that were being taken out from Walowa. And of course, some of the boys that were with me-- we were about 100 boys in that group-- encountered their relatives, their mothers, their fathers, being taken out the other way, towards the trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so they-- you know, they- they tried to communicate with them, tried-- one of them tried to jump out and embrace them and say goodbye to them. And he got shot immediately, as well as his parents. We saw sights of dozens of baby carriages with babies in them dead, killed, shot, mothers holding on to parts of the carriages in a deadly grip. I remember seeing one elderly woman. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She was sitting on a suitcase in a pool of blood. She was bleeding all over. And she wasn't dead. And she was cursing God. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And she was pleading for someone to come and kill her, because she was in tremendous pain. And for about a half hour, the time it took for us-- and we were trotting. We were not walking, because the guard told us to keep our heads down and trot.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a German guard?

speaker: interviewee
text: Those were Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ukrainian guard.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. We witnessed all of these things as we passed by. And the field probably held [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] hundreds of dead bodies.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was in control of the people who were going in columns with the deportation?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know-- yes. Well, the SS was in control. But they had-- they had guards. They had young guards. And I remember-- it's a good question you asked, because I want to bring that out. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As we passed the columns that were on their way to the trains, we also passed their guards that were guarding the columns. And what struck me at the time was the fresh-faced, clean-looking, young boys that were guarding these columns. Those where the so-called Einsatzgruppen who came for special action. And I remember looking at them. And I remember the-- that they were youngsters, very young, very blond, very clean-looking, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] with their helmets and the chin straps and the backpacks and their ready guns, as if on a holy mission--

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what was their facial expression?

speaker: interviewee
text: --in a combat. Like it was a combat-- it was a combat duty, like. And their facial expression was you either do it or you're dead. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember once in Pionki, I had befriended a young boy named Becker, a German young boy named Becker. And we were very good friends for a while. And at the time, I remember thinking, is this young Becker among these Germans? [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I remembered another thing. When I wrote this memoir, I remembered reading a statement by Reagan at the time of the Bitburg controversy where he said that there are no Germans left who had taken part in these things. And I just couldn't stand it. Evidently he already had Alzheimer's then, because, you know, I remember looking at these youngsters, and they were not much older than 16, 17, or 18 years old. And there was no way in the world that these people didn't survive to this day, because they weren't much older than I was at the time, maybe two, three years older. And yet he had the gall to say that there are no Germans alive who had participated in these things. And he said that in-- in the 1980s, in the mid-1980s.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.
